docs.title: What is it?
docs.text: Full-stack web development refers to the practice of working on both the front-end (client-side) and back-end (server-side) portions of an application.

docs.title: Roadmap to learning it
docs.text:
I use this roadmap to learn it,

  • HTML
  • CSS
  • JavaScript
  • NPM
  • Tailwind CSS
  • React
  • Node.js
  • ...

docs.title: Front-End 101
docs.text:
Front-end is all the client-side infrastructure, including the design of the site, and it's behavior.

The front-end is made up of three core languages,

  • HTML
  • CSS
  • JavaScript

On top of those languages, people made frameworks to make the coding in those languages (or environments) easier,

  • Tailwind CSS

docs.title: HTML 101
docs.text: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the most essential framework of the internet, dictating both the structure and semantic meaning of web content.

docs.title: Tailwind CSS 101
docs.text: Tailwind CSS is a utility-first CSS framework, which gives the developer access to a collection of classes that can be composed to build designs directly in HTML.

docs.title: How to install
docs.text:
To install Tailwind, we should use the NPM packet manager in our project,

npm install tailwindcss @tailwindcss/cli

docs.title: How to use
docs.text:
As said, Tailwind builds for us the CSS for the project, given an instruction set of Tailwind's commands. For example this command to import tailwindcss,

@import "tailwindcss";

Then, to create the CSS of our project from the Tailwind's , we need to use the following npx command,

npx @tailwindcss/cli -i <tailwind-instructions-file> -o <css-file>

it's good practice to use an npm script for this operation.

Now to actually use the CSS provided by Tailwind, you can use the following syntax,

<tag class="tailwind-class-1 tailwind-class-2 tailwind-class-3 ...">
    ...
</tag>

those classes can be found by searching the docs at the official site, or in this cheat sheet.

docs.title: Integration with IDE
docs.text: To leverage the Tailwind framework to it's maximum it's expected of you to use the plugin for Tailwind in visual studio code, Tailwind CSS IntelliSense.

docs.title: NPM 101
docs.text: NPM (Node Package Manager) is a JavaScript package manager (like PIP for Python).

docs.title: How to install
docs.text: NPM comes preinstalled with Node.js.

docs.title: How to use
docs.text:
To check the version of NPM you can use this,

npm -v
npm --version

To create a new project you need to create a new configuration file, meaning a new package.json file.

docs.title: Download a dependency
docs.text:
To download a new library (module) that is needed as a dependency of the final project,

npm install --save <module-name>

the --save flag is essential since it saves the module in the package.json as well.

Now given a package.json file, we can download all the dependencies needed using,

npm install

docs.title: Delete a dependency
docs.text:
To delete a dependency from the project,

npm uninstall --save <module-name>
npm un --save <module-name>
npm remove --save <module-name>
npm rm --save <module-name>

Again, the --save flag is essential since it deletes the module from the package.json as well.

docs.title: Download a development dependency
docs.text:
To download a new library (module) that is not needed as a dependency of the final project,

npm install --save-dev <module-name>

the --save-dev flag is essential since it saves the module in the package.json as well.

Note that by default when someone installs the dependencies of the project, it will also install the development dependencies, to disable that we can use,

npm install --production

docs.title: Delete a development dependency
docs.text:
To delete a development dependency from the project,

npm uninstall --save-dev <module-name>
npm un --save-dev <module-name>
npm remove --save-dev <module-name>
npm rm --save-dev <module-name>

Again, the --save-dev flag is essential since it deletes the module from the package.json as well.

docs.title: Dependency Version
docs.text:
To control the version of a dependency, we can use the following,

npm install --save <module-name>@<version>

or use --save-dev.

To update a dependency we can use,

npm update <module-name>

docs.title: List all dependencies
docs.text:
To list all the dependencies of a project,

npm list

And to see only the first level depth of dependencies,

npm list --depth 0

docs.title: Global Module
docs.text:
All the dependencies until now were tied to the project, but if you want a module to be used for all projects on your machine, you may use,

npm install -g <module-name>

And to see where those global modules are being saved you can use,

npm root -g

docs.title: Where are the libraries coming from?
docs.text: All the libraries live in this domain, npmjs.com, and you can find there a lot of packages to download.

docs.title: NPX
docs.text: NPX (Node Package Execute) is a command-line tool bundled with NPM that lets you execute JavaScript packages directly from the npm registry without needing to install them globally first. It simplifies running one-off commands and scaffolding projects.

docs.title: package.json file
docs.text: This is the project main dependency file, it tells NPM the configuration of your project.

docs.title: Creating the file
docs.text:
To create the file, you need to run this command,

npm init

if you want all the default settings without the CLI asking you each field, you may run one of the following,

npm init -y
npm init --yes

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
This file contains all the configuration for the project, and the default file looks like,

{
    "name": "fullstack",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "description": "",
    "main": "index.js",
    "scripts": {
        "test": "echo \"Error: no test specified\" && exit 1"
    },
    "author": "",
    "license": "ISC",
    "type": "commonjs"
}

docs.title: npm scripts
docs.text:
The only field that isn't self explanatory is scripts, this field is used to run bash commands, if we run,

npm run <script-name>

it will run the command in the file.

For example, if we have,

"scripts": {
    "hello": "echo \"World!\""
}

then,

>>> npm run hello
World!

docs.title: Changing the default
docs.text:
To change the default fields used by NPM, you need to use one of the following commands,

npm config set init-<field> "<new-default>"
npm set init-<field> "<new-default>"

for example,

npm config set init-author-name "John Doe"
npm set init-licence "MIT"

To get the default value,

npm config get init-<field>
npm get init-<field>

And to revert the default value,

npm config delete init-<field>
npm delete init-<field>
